focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

By the third morning after the regatta, Ferrymouth had learned the name and glowed like a rich man's tooth.

The light in Junie's kitchen had gone pale gold in the night and stayed there, thin and flattering and useless. It made the steam above the stockpot look expensive. It made yesterday's grease on the stove lids shine like lacquer. It made the floorboards look drier than they were, which was the mean part. The boards still sweated river damp, and when Silas put his bad wrist against the prep table to lever himself up from the bench, his palm came away with flour pasted to it and a smear of yellowish slime from the lamp hook.

Junie had been up long enough for anger to settle into method. She stood over the range in her apron and underskirt, hair pinned high with a trussing skewer because she could not find the proper comb in the dark. Steam had dampened the little healed split in her eyebrow white. She kept lifting the lid off the stew and glaring into the pot, trying to bully the boil back by eyesight alone.

"It won't hold," she said.

Silas knew she was not talking to him in the first instance. She was talking to the firebox, to the pale-gold cakes stacked in the coal cradle, to Gideon Wren somewhere up on clean Gilt Crescent with his gloves and his sermon voice and his purchased city. Even so, the sentence landed on Silas because most things did now.

He crossed to the stove. The heat coming off it was wrong. There was warmth, yes, enough to comfort the skin if all you wanted from a stove was an agreeable hand. It did not have the hard push of kitchen fuel. It did not get under the pot and commit.

Junie thumped the ladle against his knuckles when he reached for the fire door. "You'll spill it."

"I was looking."

"You can look with your eyes."

prev_tail_excerpt:
...rrections. The city had kept the bright part and started dropping the cost almost at once. One trouble lived in the mouth, another in the weather, and both of them wanted rougher answers than elegance.

Mara gathered her shawl. "One bowl," she said. "And now half the night. You're all thieves."

"Stay for cake," Junie said.

Mara hesitated just long enough to indict herself. "A small piece."

Damaris laughed through her nose. "Coalition achieved."

"Don't call it that in the paper," Junie said.

"I won't call it anything till we know which parts survive daylight."

That, too, felt correct. Nobody in the room reached for victory. Gideon still had money, walls, songs, debt papers, runners. Here they stayed at the table and kept dividing the work.

Junie cut the cake. Inez took the slip down from the window before the damp could curl it useless. Aunt Vero began listing which wash lines in Sump Ward saw the most dawn traffic and which ones belonged to women who would rather bite through tin than help Patent Row by accident. Damaris sorted spoiled proofs by size and muttered about paste, slats, and type weight. Mara counted stair landings on her fingers and argued about where correction would have to stand in person before anything got posted. Otis, fully awake now, asked whether sign slats could whistle if the gaps were thin enough. Junie said everything in this city whistled if the wind found the right crack.

Silas put a new sheet on the table and, before he wrote a single phrase worth painting, listed the mouths and places that would have to carry it. Beside him, Inez tore off a narrower scrap and copied three released names with their uses in her compact hand for morning's work. The rest could sprawl. What went into a pocket had to survive first contact with the street.
